title: "Understanding Astro's islands architecture"
description: "Partial hydration, client directives and a JavaScript budget: the complete guide to islands."

Astro renders everything as static HTML by default and only hydrates the components that need it. This post, deliberately long, also serves to test the table of contents.

## The problem with SPAs

A single-page application ships all of its JavaScript, even to display static text. On a blog, that makes no sense: 95% of the content is inert.

### The real cost of JavaScript

Every kilobyte of JS costs you three times: download, parsing, execution. On a mid-range phone, 300 KB of JS can block the main thread for more than a second.

### What Core Web Vitals measure

- LCP: the paint of the largest visible element
- INP: interaction latency
- CLS: visual stability

## Islands in practice

An island is an interactive component hydrated independently from the rest of the page.

### Client directives

```astro
---
import ThemeToggle from "../components/ThemeToggle";
import Comments from "../components/Comments";
---
<ThemeToggle client:load />
<Comments client:visible />
```

`client:load` hydrates immediately, `client:visible` waits until the component enters the viewport, `client:idle` waits until the browser is idle.

### When to pick what

| Directive | Use case | Priority |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `client:load` | Theme switcher, interactive header | High |
| `client:idle` | Search bar | Medium |
| `client:visible` | Bottom-of-page carousel | Low |
| `client:only` | SSR-incompatible component | Special |

## Content collections

Content lives in typed collections:

```ts
import { defineCollection, z } from "astro:content";
import { glob } from "astro/loaders";

const blog = defineCollection({
  loader: glob({ pattern: "**/*.mdx", base: "../../shared/content/blog" }),
  schema: z.object({
    title: z.string().max(70),
    description: z.string().max(160),
    pubDate: z.coerce.date(),
    updatedDate: z.coerce.date().optional(),
    tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
    draft: z.boolean().default(false),
  }),
});

export const collections = { blog };
```

### Querying the collection

```ts
const posts = (await getCollection("blog"))
  .filter((post) => !post.data.draft)
  .sort((a, b) => b.data.pubDate.valueOf() - a.data.pubDate.valueOf());
```

## Going further

### On-demand rendering

Astro can also do per-route SSR when a page needs fresh data.

### View transitions

Astro's transition router animates the move from one page to the next without a SPA, preserving the theme state.

## Conclusion

A well-built Astro blog ships a few kilobytes of JavaScript where a SPA ships hundreds. The islands architecture is not an optimization, it is a structural choice.
